title_en: Ed Zitron Gets Paid to Love AI. He Also Gets Paid to Hate AI
title_zh: 埃德·齐特龙靠爱 AI 赚钱，也靠恨 AI 赚钱
rubric_en: He’s one of the loudest voices of the AI haters—even as he does PR for AI companies. Either way, Ed Zitron has your attention.
rubric_zh: 他是AI反对者中最响亮的声音之一——尽管他替AI公司做公关。无论哪一种，都让人无法忽视埃德·齐特龙。

paragraphs.en: In his day job, Ed Zitron runs a boutique public relations firm called EZPR. This might surprise anyone who has come to know Zitron through his podcast or his social media or the newsletter in which he writes two-fisted stuff like “Sam Altman is full of shit" and “Mark Zuckerberg is a putrid ghoul.” Flacks, as a rule, tend not to talk like this. Flacks send prim, throat-clearing emails to media people who do, on rare occasions, talk like this. Flacks want to touch base, hop on the phone, clear up a few things about the allegation that their CEO is a “chunderfuck.”
paragraphs.zh: 在本职工作中，埃德·齐特龙经营着一家名为 EZPR 的精品公关公司。任何通过他的播客、社交媒体，或他那本会写出“Sam Altman is full of shit”（萨姆·阿特曼满嘴胡话）和“Mark Zuckerberg is a putrid ghoul”（马克·扎克伯格是个腐烂的食尸鬼）这类措辞的通讯来了解齐特龙的人都会感到惊讶。公关人士通常不会这样说话。公关人士会给媒体人员发礼貌、清嗓子后再说的邮件，而这些媒体人员偶尔会讲得更直白。公关人士想要对接、打个电话，把“其首席执行官是个什么狗屁东西”这类指控中的一些细节澄清清楚。

paragraphs.en: "And that really is one of the things with guys like Sam Altman and Dario Amodei from Anthropic,” Zitron was saying over burgers on a fine Manhattan afternoon in September. “I work with founders all the time. I’m a founder myself, I guess—I don’t like the title. But when you are a person that has to make more money than you lose, otherwise you lose your business, and you see these chunderfucks burning 5, 10 billion dollars in a year—and everyone's celebrating them? It's offensive.”
paragraphs.zh: “这也正是像萨姆·阿特曼和 Anthropic 的达里奥·阿莫德伊这类人存在的问题，”齐特龙在九月一个晴朗的曼哈顿午后一边吃汉堡一边说。“我经常和创始人打交道。算是个创始人，我想——我不喜欢这个称呼。可当你是那种必须赚更多钱才不至于亏损、否则就会失去自己业务的人，而你又看到这些‘混账玩意’一年内烧掉 5 亿到 100 亿美元（即 50 亿到 100 亿美元）——结果大家都在为他们庆祝？这很冒犯。”

paragraphs.en: We were talking about whether any of Zitron’s ranting about the AI industry had cost him business on the PR side of the ledger. He said no. There was the one client who felt Zitron was being a little mean toward Altman, the CEO of OpenAI and the biggest chunderfuck of all, as far as Zitron is concerned. Founding a company is hard, the client said. “I said, ‘I appreciate the comment, but, like, this isn't about you,’” Zitron told me. “His company is burning billions of dollars. He's a terrible businessman.”
paragraphs.zh: 我们在聊齐特龙对 AI 行业的这些咆哮是否影响了他在公关方面的生意。他说没有。有一个客户觉得齐特龙对奥特曼（OpenAI 的首席执行官，也是齐特龙心目中最大的“恶性废物”）有点太狠。成立一家企业很难，客户说。齐特龙对我说：“我说过，‘我感谢你的评论，但这件事跟你无关。’”他接着说，“他的公司正在烧掉数十亿美元。可他是个糟糕的生意人。”

paragraphs.en: It was, in all, a very Ed Zitron sort of riff, pitched in the key of personal affront, populist in the manner of a small business owner stink-eyeing the unpunished wastefulness of big industry. (Would these CEOs be any less offensive, one wonders, if their companies were making billions of dollars?) He has built an improbable little empire for himself out of tart commentary like this. His weekly podcast, Better Offline, about “the tech industry's influence and manipulation of society,” has cracked Spotify’s top 20 among tech shows, and his newsletter, Ed Zitron’s Where’s Your Ed At, has grown north of 80,000 subscribers. The Ed Zitron media experience also includes a scrappy Bluesky account, a football podcast, some occasional baseball writing, a lot of to-and-froing with the users of r/BetterOffline, and a book due next year about, as he puts it, “why everything stopped working.” In other media, he has become a go-to source for AI naysaying. When Slate’s What’s Next: TBD podcast or WNYC’s On the Media needed someone to talk about the bursting of the AI bubble, they called on Zitron. It isn’t just the volume of output that has put him on the map; it is the aggrieved style that he brings to criticisms of media figures and industry titans alike.
paragraphs.zh: 总体而言，这完全就是典型的齐特龙式辩驳，带着个人被冒犯的情绪基调，像个小企业主一样，以“对大公司无所惩罚的浪费行为”投来不屑的冷眼，带有一种小人物式的民粹批判色彩。（有人会问，如果他们公司的盈利也高达数十亿美元，这些首席执行官是否会少点让人反感？）他凭借这种尖刻的评论为自己构建了一个看似不可能的小帝国。

paragraphs.en: Two WIRED editors peer into their crystal ball—and glimpse humanity’s post-phone, post-screen salvation.
paragraphs.zh: 两位《连线》编辑凑在水晶球前一望，竟看见了人类在“无手机、无屏幕”之后的救赎。

paragraphs.en: Not long ago, volume and style came together to produce the quintessential bit of Zitron media: a piece for his newsletter titled “How to Argue With an AI Booster.” It was 15,000 words long.
paragraphs.zh: 不久之前，风格与篇幅终于凑在一起，诞生了最具齐特伦风格的典型之作：他给通讯订阅者写的一篇题为《如何与 AI 推广者辩论》的文章，长达 15,000 字。

paragraphs.en: Edheads abound now. Nearly 200 people have purchased a $24 Better Offline challenge coin, engraved with what has become the Zitron mantra: “NEVER FORGIVE THEM FOR WHAT THEY'VE DONE TO THE COMPUTER.” I have seen someone put Ed’s words on a motivational poster, operating at some ambiguous register of irony. One Threads user described her “parasocial crush on a tech critic & writer” who is not named but who is quite obviously Zitron. “I just want him to take me to dinner, take me gently but firmly by the hand, and tell me in his confusing, muddled British accent to throw away my goddamn phone,” she sighed. “This would fix me. I’m sure of it.” (As one tech journalist who’d seen the Threads post put it to me, “If you’re getting to a point where your writing is causing people to lust after you, you’re doing something either very right or very wrong.”)
paragraphs.zh: “艾德族”如今已不少了。已有近 200 人购买了价值 24 美元的 Better Offline 挑战币，上面刻着如今已成齐特伦口号的一句话："NEVER FORGIVE THEM FOR WHAT THEY'VE DONE TO THE COMPUTER。" 我还见过有人把艾德的话做成激励海报，语气处在某种含混的反讽层级里。一个 Threads 用户描述她对一位“科技批评者和作家”的拟社会式迷恋，这位人物未被点名，但显然是齐特伦。“我只想让他带我去吃晚饭，牵着我的手，温柔却坚定地带我离开，告诉我用他那种让人困惑、混乱的英式口音把我该死的手机扔掉。”她叹道。“这样就能拯救我，我非常确定。”（正如一位看过该帖子并在向我转述的科技记者所说，“如果你的写作让人开始对你产生欲望，那你要么做对了事，要么做了件非常糟糕的事。”）

paragraphs.en: As a functional matter, Zitron is meeting a demand for an equal-and-opposite voice to counter the inescapable AI hype. Critics of AI approach from any number of angles. There are doomers who fear the industry is ushering in some world-shattering superintelligence; there are denialists who don’t believe AI will ever replace human decisionmakers. Zitron is up to something different. What he offers people, in a time of amoral boosterism and amid a free-floating revulsion for the tech industry, is a moral language for hating generative AI. “He approaches the subject like a journalist in that he’s ravenous for information, but he is unshackled by the institutions,” says Allison Morrow, a business reporter at CNN and a frequent guest on Better Offline. “Most journalists don’t want to root for an industry’s demise. The institutions we work for don’t want to be engaged in that kind of mission.”
paragraphs.zh: 在实际层面上，齐特伦正在满足一种对立且对等的声音需求，用以抵消无法逃避的 AI 热潮。AI 的批评者可从许多角度切入。有人悲观到认为该产业正在引入会颠覆世界的超级智能；也有人否认论者，坚称 AI 永远无法取代人的决策者。齐特伦走的是另一条路。在一种缺乏道德边界的过度吹捧与对科技行业游移不定的厌恶之间，他给人们提供的是一套“可以憎恨生成式 AI 的道德语言”。“他像记者一样来这个议题时，对信息有强烈渴求，但又不受机构束缚，”CNN 的商业记者阿利森·莫罗（Allison Morrow）说，她也是 Better Offline 的常客。“大多数记者不愿意公开为某个行业的衰落鼓掌。我们所在的机构也不愿卷入这种任务。”

paragraphs.en: Maybe more importantly, for his readers and listeners, Zitron holds out the seductive promise of some great comeuppance for the industry. Justice, of some kind, for an audience that isn’t seeing much of it in evidence anywhere. “I do not think this is a real industry,” he has written, “and I believe that if we pulled the plug on the venture capital aspect tomorrow it would evaporate.” When On the Media asked how he could be so certain that a collapse was coming, he replied, “I feel it in my soul.” So his analysis may wobble here and there on the abstruse particulars of, say, inference costs. He will not be deterred from his overall message: Judgment Day is just over the horizon. Somewhat lost in all the frog-raining opprobrium is the obvious contradiction of his work, which Zitron doesn’t hide but which he rarely discusses: that he makes a living, in part, from trying to gin up attention for AI companies. Can a flack be a prophet on the side?
paragraphs.zh: 对他的读者和听众来说，更重要的是，齐特隆为这个行业许下了诱人的期待：某种正义终将到来，给这些几乎到处都看不到铁证的读者一个交代。“我不认为这是一门真正的产业，”他写道，“我相信如果我们明天就拔掉风险投资这一插头，它就会消失。”当《On the Media》问他如何如此确信崩盘即将到来时，他回应说，“我心里有这种感觉。”因此他在某些晦涩细节上，如推理成本的计算，分析可能会有些摇摆，但他始终不会被自己核心观点所动摇：审判日就在眼前。在他满是咒骂式怒火的言论里，埋藏着一个显而易见的矛盾——这个矛盾他并不隐瞒，只是几乎从不提及：他一部分生计就是为AI公司吸引注意力。一个会替AI公司打广告的人，能同时还是“预言家”吗？

paragraphs.en: Today, Zitron divides his time between New York and Las Vegas. He is 39, having spent the first half of his life in England and the latter in the United States, developing along the way a taste for such raffish but doomed Americana as minor-league baseball and the Las Vegas Raiders. But in many ways the Cassandra of the AI boom was shaped by his adolescence in London, where he grew up practically friendless, a “very fat and not particularly bright child,” who had the misfortune of attending a school known for theater kids and smarties. “From pretty much the moment I walked in to the moment I walked out,” he says, “I was bullied.”
paragraphs.zh: 如今，齐特隆的时间一半在纽约、一半在拉斯维加斯。他今年39岁，人生前半段在英格兰度过，后半段在美国，因此在这途中也培养出对次文化却注定没落的美国元素的兴趣，比如小联盟棒球和拉斯维加斯突袭者队。但在许多方面，AI热潮中的“卡珊德拉”这一角色，是由他在伦敦的青春期所塑造的：他在那里几乎没有朋友，一个“非常胖也不算聪明的孩子”，而且不幸就读于一所以戏剧爱好者和“学霸”闻名的学校。’“我几乎从一进校、到离开学校的每一刻，”他说，“都在被欺负。”

paragraphs.en: What friends Zitron did have as a kid, he found via the computer. His dad had a laptop and a PCMCIA card, and Zitron would dial up and play Ultima Online and EverQuest, finding himself in mIRC chat rooms devoted to the games. “My initial experience with the internet was just kind of wonderment, but also wonderment at the chaos,” he says. “It wasn’t like I thought, ‘This is perfect.’” He was amazed at all the “real freaks” who’d exiled themselves there, interacting in a space that, unlike other realms of life, seemed “very egalitarian.”
paragraphs.zh: 他童年时期真正交到的朋友，多是通过电脑结识的。他父亲有一台笔记本和一张PCMCIA卡，齐特隆会拨号上网，玩《Ultima Online》与《EverQuest》，并在mIRC聊天室里邂逅和这些游戏相关的玩家。“我刚接触互联网时，感到的是一种惊奇，也夹杂着对混乱的惊奇，”他说。“我并没有想过‘太完美了’。”他对那些自我放逐进去的“真·怪人”感到惊讶，在那个世界里，与现实生活中的其他领域不同，那里似乎“非常平等”。

paragraphs.en: Here, in the loneliness of a kid making connections through technology that he couldn’t forge in person, a sort of tech fetishism took root. It informs his work even today: the notion that tech drives social processes, not the other way around; that it is, in its very circuits, sociality itself. In this light, it isn’t hard to understand where his well-developed sense of personal outrage comes from. To misuse tech in some way, to exploit it or undermine it for personal or class gain, is to deny people the thing he’d found in those chat rooms with the freaks: the possibility of social connection. It is to be something like that very worst of things, a bully. When he says “never forgive them for what they’ve done to the computer,” it sounds a lot like “never forgive them for what they’ve done to me.”
paragraphs.zh: 就在这样一个孩子通过技术建立联系却无法在现实中建立联系的孤独中，一种科技崇拜开始生根。时至今日，它仍然影响着他的创作：即技术推动社会进程，而不是反过来；技术在其电路结构中本身就蕴含着社会性。在这种理解框架下，他那成熟的个人愤怒从何而来并不难理解。以某种方式误用技术、为个人或阶层利益去利用、削弱它，就是剥夺人们在那些“怪人”聊天室里他曾找到的东西：社会连接的可能性。这是一种最糟糕的行为之一，像极了欺凌者。 当他说“永远不要原谅他们对计算机所做的一切”时，这听起来很像“永远不要原谅他们对我所做的一切”。

paragraphs.en: The once-friendless Zitron now talks warmly of his many friendships, of his good mates and his dear mates and his best mates. During CES in Las Vegas, Zitron recorded his podcast from a makeshift studio in a hotel room, 13 and a half hours in all, guest after guest after guest popping by as if it were the Dean Martin Show. CES was a triumph for Zitron. Thanking some of the people in the room near the end of his final episode, Zitron began to choke up a little. “I was telling everyone how much I love them,” he tells me, only a little embarrassed. “I think we need way more of that in fucking media. We need more friendship.”
paragraphs.zh: 这位过去不大善交友的齐特伦如今常热情谈起自己广泛的朋友圈：好朋友、挚友、最好的朋友。2019年在拉斯维加斯的消费电子展（CES）期间，齐特伦在酒店房间里临时搭建录音棚，连续录制了他13个半小时的播客节目，嘉宾一位接一位地到场，像在《迪恩·马丁秀》里走红毯一样。这次 CES 对齐特伦来说是一次成功。接近最后一集结束时，他在感谢现场几位人员时，语气里带着些许哽咽。“我一直在告诉在场每个人我有多喜欢他们，”他对我说着，略带尴尬，“我想我们在媒体里需要更多这种东西。我们需要更多友情。”

paragraphs.en: It was how he approached PR. Zitron had come to the industry in 2008, having studied communications at Penn State and completed a brief tour back in London writing about video games for PC Zone. He worked for a New York City PR firm, unhappily, then as the communications director for Hometalk, a startup social platform for home improvement. He made a point of cultivating relationships with media types. He said as much in his first book, This Is How You Pitch: How to Kick Ass in Your First Years of PR. “Getting to know your contacts on a personal level is a great way to make friends out of the people—like bloggers and journalists—who cover the industry. Let them be the one to bring up your field or your client. It will make you appear human, rather than just another hungry flack looking to get a story planted.”
paragraphs.zh: 这也是他进入公关领域的方式。齐特伦于2008年进入这个行业，曾在宾州州立大学学习传播，后来回到伦敦，在《PC Zone》写了一段时间电子游戏相关内容。随后他在纽约一家公关公司工作，心情并不愉快；之后又担任 Hometalk 的传播总监，这是一家面向家居改造的初创社交平台。他有意识地与媒体人建立关系。他在第一本书《这样才能赢得开局：如何在公关生涯早期立于不败之地》里也表达过这一点：“在个人层面了解你的联系人，是把行业里的博主和记者等人变成朋友的最佳方法之一。让他们自己先提起你的行业或你的客户。这样会让你显得更有人情味，而不是像另一个只想把新闻“塞”进去的饥饿打工仔。”

paragraphs.en: It comes off so cynical there on the page, but maybe that was only for effect. Tech bloggers who can’t remember having ever been pitched by Zitron recall drinking with him at New York City bars in the 2010s, a guy on the scene but not on the make. In his book, Zitron marveled, “Some of these people may end up going to your wedding,” and indeed that’s what happened. John Herrman, the indispensable tech columnist at New York magazine, was there at Zitron’s first wedding. In attendance at his second wedding, in 2017, were a number of media people with the mordant sensibilities that would come to characterize Zitron’s own writing and podcasting later on: Chapo Trap House’s Felix Biederman, the acid polemicist Jeb Lund, sharp-eyed tech journos Mike Isaac and Sarah Emerson.
paragraphs.zh: 放在纸面上看，这似乎带着几分世故讽刺，但也许那只是故意营造的效果。那些回想起曾经并不记得自己受过齐特伦“推介”的科技博主，仍记得在2010年代于纽约酒吧与他喝酒的场景——他更像是在场的人，而不是“精心包装”的那种人。齐特伦在书中惊叹道：“其中一些人以后可能会来参加你的婚礼。”事实证明果然如此。纽约杂志里不可或缺的科技专栏作家约翰·赫尔曼就在齐特伦的第一场婚礼上出席。2017年他第二次婚礼时，出席嘉宾中有不少带着辛辣文风的人，这种风格后来也成为齐特伦本人写作和播客的标志：有《Chapo Trap House》的费利克斯·比德曼、犀利的激辩者杰布·伦德、眼光敏锐的科技记者迈克·艾萨克和萨拉·爱默森。

paragraphs.en: In 2012, Zitron the flack went indie. He was pretty good at this PR stuff, too. I know this because Forbes published a thousand-word story the next year about just how good he is at this PR stuff. In fact, some of his best message-shaping work was done on behalf of the brand known as Ed Zitron. The field of PR was a “fetid shit-pile,” he told one reporter in 2014. Zitron wanted people to know he was different. One year, he pranked his PR colleagues who were blasting out pitches in advance of CES, asking each to send him more information “via Updog.” He explained to Newsweek: “The first one to respond was, ‘I'm sorry, what's Updog?’ The glint in my eye that is just despising most of my industry is like, ‘Oh, nothing much, what's up with you?’ Screenshot, post.”
paragraphs.zh: 2012年，齐特伦这位“发言人”走向了独立。他在公关这项工作上也相当拿手。我知道这一点，是因为次年《福布斯》刊登了一篇一千字的报道，专门讲他在公关上的出色能力。事实上，他最出色的一些舆论引导作品，实际上是为“Ed Zitron”这个品牌本身所做的。2014年，他对一位记者说过，公关行业是一堆“臭烂粪堆”。齐特伦想让人们知道自己与众不同。2013年，他恶作剧地让一些正在 CES 前忙着群发宣传稿的公关同事，用“Updog”向他补充更多信息。他向《新闻周刊》解释道：“第一个回复的是：‘对不起，什么是 Updog？’ 我眼中的那种轻蔑几乎看得到——对我来说这行的主流几乎全是这样——它就像在说：‘没什么，就是想问问你最近咋样？’ 然后截图，发帖。

paragraphs.en: Zitron would write the odd piece on a freelance basis, often about PR, but it wasn’t until 2020 that he began to turn himself into a regular commentator. He started a Substack, writing at first about personal branding and other business issues, then about the fight over remote work, finding a voice and an audience for himself in clarifying the debate as a sort of class war within the office. In 2023, he wrote “The Rot Economy,” which attributed the derelictions of Big Tech to the appetite for “eternal growth at the cost of the true value of any given service or entity.” “That was when I'm like, I should try and divine meaning from all these things,” Zitron says.
paragraphs.zh: Zitron曾经偶尔以自由撰稿人的方式写一些文章，多半是关于公关的，但直到2020年他才开始把自己转型为一位稳定发声的评论者。他创建了一个 Substack，起初主要写个人品牌和其他商业议题，后来又写了关于远程办公之争，以“办公室内的阶级战争”来梳理这场争论，从而为自己找到了一种发声方式和受众。2023年，他写下了《腐败经济》（The Rot Economy），将大科技企业的失职归因于其对“以永续增长为代价、牺牲任何服务或组织真实价值”的偏好。“那时我就想，也许我该尝试从这些现象中读出一些意义，”Zitron说。

paragraphs.en: The AI boom, and especially the follies of Sam Altman and OpenAI, offered the ideal subject for him. “I think Sam has more character than most of the Valley people,” Zitron tells me. “Doesn’t mean I like him, but he’s an operator. He’s good at it. And he’s very clearly good at”—and here a note of genuine admiration creeps in—“making enough friends but also exerting enough force to keep enemies away.” Altman isn’t an engineer. He is a “carnival barker,” in Zitron’s phrase, a man pushing “gobbledygook, nonsense, bullshit." Who better to criticize a creature of hype than a professional hype man?
paragraphs.zh: AI热潮，尤其是Sam Altman和OpenAI的一连串荒唐举动，为他提供了理想的题材。“我认为Sam比硅谷大多数人更有个性，”Zitron对我说。“这并不代表我喜欢他，但他是个真正会干事的人。”他又补充说：“并且他很明显做得很好——”这时语气里透出了一丝真诚的钦佩——“既能交够多朋友，也能施加足够压力把敌人挡在外面。”Altman并非工程师。在Zitron看来，他更像一个“马戏团叫卖人”，一个不断推销“术语堆砌、空洞言论、胡说八道”的人。谁比专业的“煽动专家”更适合去批评一个靠造势吸金的“生物”呢？

paragraphs.en: Zitron has been fascinated by tech since he was a kid.
paragraphs.zh: Zitron从小就对科技着迷。

paragraphs.en: Zitron was a blogger now, doing enjoyable bloggy things like hanging rude epithets on CEOs and antagonizing the normie tech media. Kevin Roose and Casey Newton, the hosts of the New York Times’ relatively bullish Hard Fork podcast, quickly became prime targets. They’re too friendly with their subjects, says Zitron, who called Hard Fork a case study in journalists using “their power irresponsibly.” He recalls having pitched Newton once in his capacity as a flack, but nothing came of it. Newton, for his part, remembers meeting Zitron somewhere, maybe a decade ago, and Zitron saying something like, “I would really like to be friends.” Nothing came of that, either.
paragraphs.zh: 如今，Zitron成了一名博客作者，做着他喜欢的“博文式”事情，比如给CEO贴恶意绰号、反击普通的科技媒体。纽约时报相对偏乐观的《Hard Fork》播客主播Kevin Roose和Casey Newton，很快就成为他的主要目标。Zitron认为，他们对采访对象太过友好，把《Hard Fork》变成了记者滥用“话语权”的案例。还在他担任公关“替身”时，他回忆曾向Newton递过一个提案，但都没有结果。Newton也记得自己大约十年前在某个地方见过Zitron，那时Zitron说过类似“我真的很想和你做朋友”这样的话。那次也没带来任何结果。

paragraphs.en: “It's true of me that I just don't enjoy interacting with agency PR people that much,” Newton says. He calls Zitron’s recent turn to commentary “a new gimmick for himself—AI hater number one” and says Zitron has a “one-sided beef" with him. (Zitron says it’s a “critique.”) He compares Zitron’s prolix style to “some cross between the Always Sunny meme of Charlie [with the murder board] and then a prisoner smearing shit on the walls to make a point.” Newton went on: “I think Ed has actually flown too close to the sun now, because he’s built this pedestal for himself, and he’s climbed on top, and he’s posing for glossy portraits in the Financial Times magazine and WIRED, wearing aviators like the fucking Temu Kara Swisher.”
paragraphs.zh: 牛顿说：‘我就是那种根本不太喜欢和公关公司的人打交道的人。’他称赞Zitron最近转向时评是“给自己找的一个新噱头——第一号AI喷子”，并说Zitron对他有“偏向一边的积怨”。（Zitron则说这只是“批评”。）他把Zitron冗长的写作风格比作“《好汉两个半》（《It's Always Sunny》）里查理在谋杀布告板前摆弄内容的那种梗，和囚犯往墙上拉屎来表达立场的中间状态。”牛顿接着说：‘我觉得Ed现在已经飞得太接近太阳了，因为他替自己建了个神坛，爬上了顶，还在《金融时报》杂志和《连线》里摆拍名人照，戴着像该死的Temu Kara Swisher那样的墨镜。’

paragraphs.en: Brian Merchant consults the tea leaves (and tech historians) for hype levels.
paragraphs.zh: Brian Merchant会去“占卜”——他会去咨询茶叶（以及科技史学家）来判断热度水平。

paragraphs.en: As Zitron sees it, Hard Fork has sacrificed its critical faculties at the altar of friendship. Later he’ll allude to the fact—disclosed by Newton—that Newton’s boyfriend works at Anthropic. “I think that they have made friends with these people,” Zitron says. “You ever see Almost Famous? Don’t make friends with the rock stars.”
paragraphs.zh: 在Zitron看来，《Hard Fork》在友情的祭坛上牺牲了它的批判能力。随后他会提到一个细节——Newton曾披露：Newton的男友在Anthropic任职。‘我认为他们和这些人已经交了朋友。’Zitron说。‘你看过《Almost Famous》吗？别和摇滚巨星交朋友。’

paragraphs.en: Other habits of ye olde blogosphere lived on in Zitron’s work. More important than the feuding is what we might call his tech-blogger eschatology. One longtime tech writer described it for me, drawing on his own experience in the 2010s: “You see something getting shittier and shittier, worse and worse, you see your authentic engagement with the core product just sort of dying, you see a shittily rotting thing, and you have this moral instinct that, therefore, it will all come crashing down.” But the crash rarely ever comes. It didn’t come for Facebook or Twitter. For that matter it didn’t come for crypto, which after a great deal of doomsaying merely wound up buying a presidency.
paragraphs.zh: Zitron的作品里，旧博客圈的其他习惯也依然存在。更重要的是，那可以称作他的科技博客作者式末世观。一个长期的科技作家曾给我这样形容过，这话也来自他在2010年代的经历：‘你看到某样东西越变越差、越来越烂，你看到自己对核心产品的真实参与也在慢慢消失，你看到一个烂到发臭的东西，于是凭着道德直觉，觉得它迟早会轰然倒塌。’但倒塌大都不会真的发生。Facebook和Twitter都没有发生。更何况加密货币，在大量末日预言之后，最后只是买下一枚总统位。

paragraphs.en: What people were actually witnessing as they watched a particular product get worse—enshittify, to use Cory Doctorow’s coinage—was not the slow approach of some inexorable fate, as many believed. It was, as the tech writer put it to me, “evidence of a company doing very well by being very bad.” A lot of sharp people still don’t see it, in part because of a faith, not yet exhausted, in some kind of self-correcting market mechanism: A costly and degraded product would be punished somehow. It simply had to be. Forget generative AI. That faith is the biggest bubble of all.
paragraphs.zh: 当人们看着某个产品一路走下坡路——用 Cory Doctorow 的说法就是被“越搞越烂”——他们看到的并不是许多人以为的、那种缓慢而不可阻挡的命运。正如这位科技记者对我说的，这是“公司通过做得很差却做得很成功”的证据。许多聪明人仍然没有意识到这一点，部分原因在于他们仍然相信一种尚未被否定殆尽的机制：昂贵且质量下降的产品总会被市场以某种方式惩罚。这本来似乎理所当然。忘掉生成式 AI 吧。那种信念才是最大的泡沫。

paragraphs.en: “Got a really unique one here,” the email began. It was Zitron, and in August 2024 he was pitching a journalist on an EZPR client: Fulcra Dynamics, a company that collects your information, including health data, and then connects it to LLM models like ChatGPT, allowing you to “talk” to your own data, ask it questions about your workouts or the ETA of an Instacart order. Fulcra is backed by the Winklevoss twins. While some AI skeptics could see it as the sort of predatory crap that, with any luck, could be sued into the ether before everyone’s biometrics get leaked on X.com, Zitron in his email expressed no such qualms about Fulcra or its leadership.
paragraphs.zh: 这封邮件开头是“我这有个非常独特的项目”。发件人是 Zitron。2024 年 8 月，他向一位记者推介了 EZPR 的一个客户：Fulcra Dynamics。该公司会收集你的信息，包括健康数据，再把它接入像 ChatGPT 这样的 LLM 模型，让你能够“和自己的数据对话”，向它询问你的健身数据，或 Instacart 订单何时送达。Fulcra 获得了 Winklevoss 兄弟的投资。一些 AI 怀疑者会将其视为一种掠夺式伎俩，至少在理想情况下，应该在每个人的生物识别数据泄露到 X.com 之前就被人打官司逼到无处可逃；但在这封邮件里，Zitron 对 Fulcra 及其领导层没有任何这种担忧。

paragraphs.en: “These guys rock,” he wrote of the company’s founders. “I will stake my rep on them.”
paragraphs.zh: “这伙人很顶尖，”他写道，谈到该公司的创始人。“我愿意拿我的信誉去担保他们。”

paragraphs.en: As Zitron’s profile has risen, so has the delight with which some journalists pass around stories about him—the maverick scourge of AI—peddling pitches about this or that AI service, helping to inflate the bubble with one hand while poking at it with the other. One reporter I know, discomfited by Zitron’s seemingly conflicting personas, stopped replying to his messages altogether. Several journalists cited Zitron’s plumping for Nomi, which would become notorious for an incident in which one of the platform’s companions reportedly told a man to kill himself. (EZPR and Nomi have parted ways, and Zitron tells me he “won’t work with AI companions again.”) In his pitches, Zitron said there was a sex angle to the Nomi story, too, though no one I talked to pursued the idea long enough to find out what it was, exactly. “A hero for our time,” Newton scoffed in a text when he mentioned Zitron’s work with Nomi to me.
paragraphs.zh: 随着 Zitron 的知名度上升，某些记者在传播他——那个 AI 反对派的异类人物——的故事时，似乎更是乐在其中：一边兜售他写的各种 AI 服务推介，一边用一只手往泡沫里打气，另一只手又扎破泡沫。一位我认识的记者对 Zitron 看似互相冲突的身份感到不安，最后干脆不再回复他的消息。多位记者提到 Zitron 在为 Nomi 划下重金支持一笔，这家公司后来因一次事件而臭名昭著：据说其一名“陪伴”曾建议一名男子自杀。（EZPR 与 Nomi 已经分道扬镳，Zitron 也告诉我他“不会再与 AI 陪伴项目合作”。）在他的推介中，Zitron 还说 Nomi 事件里有“性”这个角度，但我采访的任何人都没有追问够久，去弄清那到底是什么。Newton 在一次短信中对我说到他与 Nomi 的合作时，嘲讐道： “我们时代的英雄。”

paragraphs.en: Zitron is an easy mark for journalists who consume both sides of his output. The gap between the eager-beaver simpering of the pitches and the spiky prose of his newsletter is laughable. There’s so much space between “these guys rock” and “these chunderfucks” that I think Meta just built a data center there. Newton feels that the AI critics deserve a better avatar. “We’re in a tribal time, and people look at [Zitron], and they think, ‘I want to be in the ‘fuck AI’ tribe,” Newton says. “There’s a lot of really great reasons to be in the ‘fuck AI’ tribe. I just think it should be led by somebody who’s not an AI publicist.” Another journalist, who broadly agrees with Zitron’s critique of the industry, laments with a sigh that “the Pied Piper of the anti-AI movement" wound up being “this guy.”
paragraphs.zh: 对同时消费他两面文字的记者来说，Zitron 简直是个“现成目标”。他在推销时那种热衷到近乎拍马屁的腔调，与他通讯稿里那种尖刻文风之间，差距大得可笑。"他们这帮人太强了"和"这些该死的废物"之间，空间大得离谱，我敢说连 Meta 都能在中间建个数据中心。Newton 认为，AI 批评者们需要一个更好的代言人。“我们现在处在部落化的时代，很多人看到 [Zitron]，就会想：‘我要加入‘反 AI’部落。’”Newton 说，“有很多非常充分的理由让你加入‘反 AI’部落。我只是觉得，应该由一个不是 AI 公关人士的人来带领它。”另一位记者同样大致认同 Zitron 对这个行业的批评，只是叹息道，反 AI 运动的“皮德·派珀”结果却成了“这位先生”。

paragraphs.en: As a voice in the tech world, Zitron has been increasingly known for his doomsaying about generative AI.
paragraphs.zh: 在科技圈中，Zitron 的声音近来因其对生成式 AI 的末日预言而愈发出名。

paragraphs.en: Today, EZPR has four clients, none of which, Zitron claims, is in the business of generative AI. “I don't want to pitch generative AI,” he says. “It's boring and shit. It sucks. I've dug into this stuff and tried to find things—like, it’s economically ruinous, it's environmentally destructive, it steals from everyone. Is there something I’m missing? No.” He exempts Nomi on the grounds that “they use their own models, and they use their own training data.”
paragraphs.zh: 截至目前，EZPR 有四位客户，他称其中没有任何一家从事生成式 AI 业务。“我不想去推销生成式 AI，”他说，“这很无聊、很烂，烂透了。我查过这些东西，也试着找了原因——经济上是毁灭性的、环境上是有害的、它还会挖走每个人的价值。有我遗漏的地方吗？没有。”他之所以豁免 Nomi，是因为“他们使用自己的模型，而且使用自己的训练数据。”

paragraphs.en: Zitron does rep AI services like DoNotPay, which once billed itself as the “world’s first robot lawyer.” He described it as “automation that can get you through bureaucracy, fill in forms, look up if you’re in a class-action suit, if you have lost money, that kind of thing.” Innocent as that description may seem, the company was hit with a complaint from the Federal Trade Commission under Lina Khan, alleging that it made “deceptive claims about the abilities of its AI chatbot.” DoNotPay and the FTC reached a six-figure settlement in which the company neither admitted nor denied the agency's claims. (Zitron had stopped working with DoNotPay before the FTC investigation, he says, though the company is now once again a client.)
paragraphs.zh: Zitron 曾为 AI 服务如 DoNotPay 做过推广，该公司曾自称为“世界上第一位机器人律师”。他将其描述为“可以帮你应对官僚程序、填写表格、查你是否卷入过集体诉讼、是否有损失了钱财之类的那种自动化工具”。这段看似无害的描述背后，Lina Khan 领导的联邦贸易委员会（FTC）对这家公司提起了投诉，指控其对 AI 聊天机器人的能力做出了“虚假误导性宣传”。DoNotPay 与 FTC 达成了一笔六位数金额的和解，和解中公司未承认也未否认监管机构的指控。（Zitron 说，在 FTC 调查前他已经与 DoNotPay 停止合作，尽管该公司现在又成了客户。）

paragraphs.en: Zitron was unbothered when I brought up his AI clientele. If his critics find in his day job evidence of a disqualifying hypocrisy or, at best, a sort of whimpery special pleading at odds with the ferocity of his overall critique, so be it. He seemed at ease in his choices. What hypocrisy, anyway? What special pleading? He works with the companies he likes, the companies he would stake his rep on, as he put it in that Fulcra pitch. Matthew Hughes, editor of Zitron’s newsletter, calls him a “technologist at heart” who is “open to a world in which … GenAI is actually good and isn’t harmful.”
paragraphs.zh: 当我提到他的 AI 客户时，齐特隆并不在意。如果他的批评者在他的本职工作中找到足以证明其“失格”的虚伪证据，或者至少找到一种与他整体猛烈批判背道而驰的软弱辩解，那就这样吧。他对自己的选择似乎很坦然。到底是什么虚伪？什么软弱的诉求？他与他看中的公司合作，与他愿意用自己的名誉作担保的公司合作，正如他在 Fulcra 的路演中所说。齐特隆通讯的编辑马修·休斯称他是“从内心热爱技术的人”，并且“对一个生成式人工智能其实是有益且并非有害的世界持开放态度。”

paragraphs.en: In truth, Zitron’s two jobs aren’t in as much tension as they might seem to be. The PR wheedling and the critical needling come from the same place: He loves this stuff. He’s just mad it doesn’t work better. “I'm actually kind of a lover,” he said. “I love my friends, and I really love the computer.” The problem is that “they’re fucking it up. They're fucking up the computer. They're making it worse. AI does not do what they say it will. The Metaverse didn't. Crypto doesn't. Just these lies. And then the products we use every day get so much worse.”
paragraphs.zh: 事实上，齐特隆的这两份工作并没看上去那么对立。那种恭维式公关和尖锐批评都来自同一个地方：他爱这些东西。他只是生气它们没有做到更好。“我其实算是个爱好者，”他说，“我爱我的朋友，也非常喜欢电脑。” 问题在于，“他们他妈的把它们搞砸了。他们把电脑搞坏了。情况只会越来越糟。AI 并没有做到它们承诺的样子。元宇宙没有做到。加密货币也没有。尽是谎言。然后，我们每天用的产品就会变得更差。”

paragraphs.en: Proudly unsnowed though he may be by the supposed propaganda of Sam Altman and the like, Zitron subscribes to a premise in line with the Promethean fantasies of the tech industry. In his schema, there is the computer, possessed of some essence, borne into the future by the tide of technological progress, and there are the they who act on it, sometimes for good, sometimes for ill. It doesn’t occur to him that the computer comes to us already shaped by the desires of the they, among others—that it is a social product, not just a cause but an effect as well.
paragraphs.zh: 尽管他可能对山姆·奥特曼等人所谓的宣传大谈特谈都很不以为然，齐特隆仍然认同科技行业中一贯的普罗米修斯式幻想。在他的框架里，存在着一台电脑，它拥有某种“本质”，在技术进步浪潮中被推向未来；还有那些操纵它的人，有时是为了善，有时是为了恶。他从未想到，这台电脑在来到我们面前之前，本就已经被“他们”——以及其他群体——的欲望塑造成形；它不仅是原因，也是结果，是一种社会产品。

paragraphs.en: That’s the funny part of Zitron’s becoming the face of tech’s new pessimism. He is, in fact, its truest believer.
paragraphs.zh: 这正是齐特隆成为科技新悲观主义代言人最有趣的地方。事实证明，他恰恰是其中最坚定的信奉者。

paragraphs.en: Whatever you do, don’t ask Katie Drummond if AI can report the news.
paragraphs.zh: 无论你做什么，都不要问凯蒂·德拉蒙德 AI 能不能报道新闻。

paragraphs.en: Zitron has a theory: This is mostly 2021’s fault. By “this” he means everything, not just the AI bubble, not just the intuition that everything in tech is getting worse, but the whole vertiginous sense that, given the central role of AI in the national economy, we’re all riding some bomb like Slim Pickens into an oblivion of our own fashioning. In 2021, companies that had hunkered down at the onset of the pandemic, anticipating a recession, now found themselves in a feast of fat things. Consumer demand for gadgets and online services was soaring, and the stock market was too. “Every number went up,” Zitron says. “Everyone was going crazy. And I think the tech companies have been chasing that growth ever since.”
paragraphs.zh: 齐特朗有一个理论：这主要是 2021 年的错。这里的“这”指的是一切，不只是 AI 热潮，不只是“科技领域一切都在变坏”的直觉，而是那种令人眩晕的整体感受：在 AI 承担国家经济核心角色的背景下，我们仿佛都像斯林·皮肯斯（Slim Pickens）那样，正骑着一辆通向由我们自己制造的末日深渊的车。2021 年，那些在疫情初期蜷缩下来、预期会发生衰退的公司，后来却发现自己进入了一场“大餐”时代。消费者对电子产品和在线服务的需求飙升，股市也同样攀升。“所有数字都在上涨，”齐特朗说。“每个人都疯了。我想，科技公司从那时起就一直在追着这种增长冲刺。”

paragraphs.en: Zitron and I were sitting in a café, having just left iHeartMedia’s disconcertingly barren Midtown Manhattan office, where he had recorded an episode of his podcast. The show had gone well, and he was riffing now as if the mic were still hot, emphasizing sundry outrages with an expression of pop-eyed incredulity.
paragraphs.zh: 有一阵子不算久以前，齐特朗对我说，在那时“激励机制还没有吞噬产品。”当时我们坐在一家咖啡馆里，刚刚离开 iHeartMedia 那间让人不安地空荡荡的曼哈顿中城办公室，他在那里录制了一期播客。节目进展顺利，他现在仍像麦克风还在开启一样侃侃而谈，带着目瞪口呆般的惊讶语气强调着各种骇人听闻的事件。

paragraphs.en: There was a time not long ago when “the incentives had not eaten the product yet,” Zitron said. “It’s why I point to 2021, because they got to see how much growth they could pull out of these things. So the evil began.”
paragraphs.zh: 齐特朗说：“并不久以前还有这么一段时间：‘激励机制还没有吞噬掉产品。’”他接着说：“这就是我为何指向 2021 年，因为他们那时看见了这些东西能榨取多少增长。所以，邪恶就从那时开始了。”

paragraphs.en: That’s one way of telling the story. It has compelling narrative elements: bad guys, greed, a golden age, a fall. It was more or less the story he told about Google in a newsletter piece last year, “The Man Who Killed Google Search.” “If you take one thing away from this newsletter, I want it to be the name Prabhakar Raghavan, and an understanding that there are people responsible for the current state of technology,” he wrote. Under Raghavan, then the company’s head of search, “Google has become less reliable, less transparent, and is dominated by search engine optimized aggregators, advertising, and outright spam.” Zitron cited the fuzzing of distinctions between organic search results and paid results. But whatever specific decisions Raghavan might’ve made, the process of subordinating Google’s search function to revenue imperatives had been set in motion years ago. The company launched its AdWords program in 2000, after all. (Per a Google spokesperson, the company will only roll out changes that they've "confirmed will improve the experience.”)
paragraphs.zh: 这正是讲述这则故事的一种方式。它具有吸引人的叙事元素：坏人、贪婪、黄金时代和陨落。这在他去年一篇通讯中几乎复刻了他对 Google 的叙述，题为《杀死 Google 搜索的人》。他写道：“如果你只记住一件事，那就是普拉巴卡尔·拉加万（Prabhakar Raghavan）的名字，并理解有人为当前的技术状态负责。”在拉加万担任当时公司的搜索负责人期间，“Google 变得更不可靠、更不透明，并且被搜索引擎优化聚合器、广告和彻底的垃圾内容所主导。”Zitron 引用了“自然搜索结果”和“付费结果”之间界限被模糊化的情况。但无论拉加万本人具体做了哪些决策，谷歌把搜索功能服从于营收优先级的进程早在数年前就已经启动。毕竟公司早在 2000 年就推出了 AdWords 计划。（根据谷歌发言人说法，谷歌只会推出“他们确认能够提升体验”的改动。）

paragraphs.en: The AI explosion was a function of the post-2021 growth drive, too, in Zitron’s telling. But that story is too simple as well. The dream of machines without humans goes back a lot further. It is older than generative AI, older than Sam Altman himself, and it is underwritten by compulsions of industrial capitalism that are older still.
paragraphs.zh: 在 Zitron 的叙述中，AI 爆发也只是 2021 年后增长驱动的一部分表现。但这个说法也太过简化。追求“没有人的机器”的梦想要早得多。它比生成式 AI 更早，甚至比萨姆·阿尔特曼本人更早，而它背后是更加久远的工业资本主义驱动因素。

paragraphs.en: “Men behaving like machines paved the way for machines without men,” David Noble wrote in Forces of Production, his classic 1984 history of industrial automation. He meant that automating work could only become thinkable once workers had been reduced to automatons—that the machine emerged from the “basic relations of domination” that shape society. The development of “capital-intensive, labor-saving, skill-reducing" machine-tool automation was a sort of social and political fix in the guise of a technological leap, a way of shoring up hierarchies in the factory, disciplining the workers, and consolidating relationships with the military, all while presenting itself as no less natural and inevitable a transition than the one from stone tools to bronze. The AI boom that Zitron reproaches grew out of these same social dynamics, and so, to some degree, did the era of technology that he nostalgizes. The computer has never been innocent of the world that made it.
paragraphs.zh: 戴维·诺布尔在其经典著作《生产力的力量》（Forces of Production）中写道：“像机器一样行为的人为机器铺平了无人的机器之路。”他是指自动化劳动之所以会被想象出来，前提是工人先被降级为自动机——机器是在塑造社会的“基本支配关系”中诞生的。“资本密集、节省劳力、降技能”的机床自动化发展，本质上是以技术飞跃的姿态进行的一种社会和政治修复：在工厂中稳固等级秩序、约束工人，并巩固与军方的关系，同时把这种转变包装成与从石器向青铜器过渡一样自然且必然的变化。Zitron 所斥责的 AI 热潮正是从这些同样的社会动力中生长出来的，因此，他怀旧的技术时代也在某种程度上由此而来。计算机从未与造就它的世界无关。

paragraphs.en: The week I met him, Zitron was feeling validated. The spirit of 2021 had beaten a retreat. Signs of an AI chill were general across the land. Meta was preparing to downsize its AI division. An MIT study had gone viral for its finding that, “despite $30–40 billion in enterprise investment into GenAI … 95% of organizations are getting zero return.” I asked Zitron what would have to happen before he could declare victory. He answered quickly: “OpenAI dying or being absorbed into Anthropic, or one of these major companies pulling capital expenditures.” Zitron thought a bit more. Wasn’t he making money as a writer, doing work he was enjoying immensely, taking it to the chunderfucks? Wasn’t he appearing on podcasts and TV as an expert in the field? Wasn’t he making a lot of friends—so many good mates and dear mates and best mates? He reconsidered. “I’ve already won.”
paragraphs.zh: 我见到他那周，Zitron 正在得到某种“证明”。2021 年的狂热气氛已经退潮。全网都出现了 AI 冷却的迹象。Meta 正在准备裁减其 AI 部门。麻省理工学院的一项研究因其发现而走红：尽管企业在生成式 AI 上投入了 300 亿到 400 亿美元……但“95% 的组织没有获得任何回报”。我问 Zitron，在什么情况下他才会宣布胜利。他很快回答： “OpenAI 倒下或被 Anthropic 吸收，或者这些大公司之一在减缓资本支出。” 他又想了想。难道他不是正在以写作者的身份挣钱，而且是在做自己非常享受的工作吗？难道他不是把这件事推广得如火如荼吗？难道他不是交了很多朋友——那么多好朋友、挚友和死党吗？他又重新考虑了一下。 “我已经赢了。”

paragraphs.zh: 请告诉我们您对这篇文章的看法。请给编辑部来信到 [email protected]。
